title: Dutch court finds automated welfare risk-scoring system incompatible with fundamental rights
date_note: Date of the Hague District Court ruling. The SyRI system was deployed and operational from approximately 2014 to 2020. The court ruling date is used as the primary record anchor.
ai_system_context: Automated risk-scoring system (SyRI — Systeem Risico Indicatie) used by Dutch government authorities to assess the risk of social welfare fraud among benefit recipients. The system combined data from multiple government databases to generate risk scores for individuals.
summary: According to the ruling of the Hague District Court of 5 February 2020 (ECLI:NL:RBDHA:2020:1878), a coalition of civil society organisations brought a legal challenge against the Dutch government's use of the SyRI automated risk-scoring system. The court found that the legal framework governing SyRI was incompatible with Article 8 of the European Convention on Human Rights, which protects the right to private life. The court found that the system lacked sufficient transparency about the logic by which risk scores were generated, making it impossible for individuals to understand or effectively challenge determinations made about them. The court also found that SyRI had been deployed predominantly in lower-income municipalities, raising concerns about disproportionate impact. The Dutch government did not appeal the ruling and subsequently discontinued the SyRI system.
evidence_required: EV-002 — Risk assessment: documented proportionality and fundamental rights impact assessment before deployment of automated scoring systems affecting benefit recipients
EV-004 — Human oversight record: evidence of human review procedures before automated risk scores are used to initiate investigations or affect individuals' entitlements
EV-014 — Post-incident review: post-ruling review of automated government decision-support systems for compliance with fundamental rights obligations
EV-012 — Bias testing record: evidence of demographic impact assessment for automated systems deployed in government welfare contexts
lessons: Automated government systems that assign risk scores to individuals must provide sufficient transparency about the factors and logic used to allow affected individuals to understand and effectively challenge outcomes.

AI systems deployed in social welfare contexts require explicit proportionality and fundamental rights impact assessments, with particular attention to disproportionate effects on lower-income or marginalised groups.
Government use of automated scoring for fraud detection or eligibility assessment requires a clear, lawful basis and must be consistent with applicable fundamental rights obligations.
Organisations should not deploy automated systems that make or inform consequential determinations about individuals without establishing adequate human oversight and review mechanisms.

sources.title: Hague District Court, ECLI:NL:RBDHA:2020:1878, 5 February 2020 — Ruling on SyRI automated welfare risk-scoring system (Dutch; key findings widely reported in English)
UN Special Rapporteur on Extreme Poverty and Human Rights — Report on the Digital Welfare State, A/HRC/41/54, October 2019 (secondary context source)

harms: Individuals subject to automated risk scoring without adequate legal basis or transparency, as found by the court
Potential for incorrect fraud-risk classification of welfare recipients in affected municipalities
affected_stakeholders: Social welfare recipients in municipalities where SyRI was deployed
Government agencies using automated risk-scoring tools
Data protection and fundamental rights authorities
impact: The Hague District Court ruled that the legal framework governing SyRI was incompatible with Article 8 ECHR. The Dutch government did not appeal the ruling. The SyRI system was discontinued following the ruling. The case is widely cited in European AI governance literature as an example of automated government decision-support systems being found incompatible with fundamental rights.
